figures.caption: Fig. 4. Distribution of cosine similarities between matched source–destination token pairs produced by standard token merging, measured separately for key tokens and value tokens.

Fig. 10. Merging vs. pruning for key-value tokens. Both strategies use the same temporal stride partitioning into source and destination tokens. Top: ATE as a function of the number of input frames; both achieve nearly identical pose error. Bottom: wall-clock token reduction time. Token merging grows superlinearly due to the bipartite similarity computation, while token pruning remains near zero throughout.

Fig. 5. Per-layer sensitivity to key-value pruning in VGGT (24 layers). Each group of bars reports the degradation ratio when a single layer’s reduction factor is raised from 32 to 256, with all other layers held at 32. The red dashed line marks the 1.05× threshold used to separate high- and low-sensitivity layers.
